title: ADK-303: Collaborative Multi-Agent Patterns
description: Master consensus protocols, voting nodes, shared blackboards, and sequential editing chains across agent workgroups.

highlights.title: Blackboards
highlights.description: Design shared state stores where multiple independent agents read and write variables during calculations.

highlights.title: Voting Consensus
highlights.description: Implement consensus checks where worker agents validate each other's outputs before writing to files.

highlights.title: Sequential Pipelines
highlights.description: Structure multi-agent pipelines (writer -> reviewer -> publisher) to automate corporate document writing.

blocks.content: In a blackboard architecture, agents do not message each other directly. Instead, they read from and write to a centralized, shared state space called the 'Blackboard'.

blocks.title: Decoupling Benefits
blocks.content: This decouples agent logic. Agents monitor changes on the blackboard and act only when their specialized inputs are ready.

Question 1: How do agents exchange information in a Blackboard architecture?
- A) Direct network sockets
- B) By posting to and reading from a shared central memory store
- C) Via command-line arguments

Feedback: Option B is correct. The Blackboard is a shared space.

modules.title: 2. Sequential processing pipelines with intermediate validation

blocks.content: A sequential pipeline passes a work artifact along a chain of agents. For example, a Writer Agent drafts text, a Reviewer Agent identifies bugs, and an Editor Agent applies corrections.

Question 1: What is the purpose of intermediate validation in sequential pipelines?
- A) To encrypt files before saving.
- B) To verify quality at each step, preventing downstream errors.
- C) To compile code modules.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Validation ensures errors are corrected early.
For safety-critical decisions, you can query multiple instances of an agent and require a consensus vote before executing a tool (like releasing funds).

blocks.code:
votes = [agent_a.evaluate(data), agent_b.evaluate(data), agent_c.evaluate(data)]
# Count yes/no votes
yes_count = sum(1 for v in votes if v == "APPROVED")
consensus_reached = yes_count >= 2

blocks.title: Latency Cost
blocks.content: Voting patterns require multiple LLM calls. Ensure the execution time does not exceed the application's timeout budgets.

Question 1: What is the primary disadvantage of multi-agent voting architectures?
- A) They are less accurate.
- B) They increase latency and API token costs because multiple LLM calls run in parallel.
- C) They require database migrations.

Feedback: Option B is correct. More calls mean higher costs and times.
When multiple agents write to the blackboard concurrently, race conditions occur. You must apply write locks, transaction scopes, or timestamp checks to maintain consistency.

Question 1: How do you prevent two agents from overwriting each other's blackboard changes concurrently?
- A) Run them sequentially only.
- B) Apply mutual exclusion locks (mutexes) or transactions to write operations.
- C) Turn off all memory variables.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Locks guarantee thread-safe writes.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Building a collaborative document drafting team

blocks.content: Lab: Building a Collaborative Document Drafting Team
In this lab, you will build a Python program that defines a sequential pipeline. You will run a Writer Agent that produces text and a Reviewer Agent that flags missing details.

Question 1: What detail was reported as missing by the Reviewer Agent?
- A) ADK installation steps
- B) Details about sandboxed execution
- C) Code indentation rules

Feedback: Option B is correct. The text draft did not contain the word 'sandbox'.
